title: Sambar
description: Sambar is a classic South Indian lentil and vegetable stew made with toor dal, tamarind, vegetables, and aromatic spices. It is a staple accompaniment for rice, idli, dosa, vada, and many other South Indian dishes.

ingredients.title: For the Tamarind Broth
items: 1 small lemon-sized ball of tamarind (or 2 tbsp tamarind paste)

ingredients.title: For Tempering (Tadka)
items: A few fresh coriander leaves, chopped (for garnish)

instruction.title: Step 1: Cook the Lentils
steps: Pressure cook with 3 cups water, ½ tsp turmeric, and a pinch of salt until soft (3–4 whistles).

instruction.title: Step 2: Prepare Tamarind Extract
steps: Soak tamarind in warm water for 15 minutes.
Squeeze and strain to get smooth tamarind water.

instruction.title: Step 3: Make Sambar Masala
steps: Dry roast coriander seeds, cumin seeds, black peppercorns, fenugreek, and dried red chilies until aromatic.
Cool and grind to a fine powder.

instruction.title: Step 4: Cook Vegetables
steps: In a saucepan, add the diced vegetables, tomato, green chilies, turmeric, and a cup of water.
Cook until vegetables are tender but not mushy.

instruction.title: Step 5: Combine
steps: Add the cooked dal and tamarind extract to the vegetables.
Mix in 1–2 tsp of freshly ground sambar masala.
Simmer for 8–10 minutes on low heat. Adjust salt and consistency (water as needed).

instruction.title: Step 6: Tempering (Tadka)
steps: Heat ghee or oil in a small pan.
Add mustard seeds; let them splutter.
Add curry leaves, dried red chilies, and asafoetida.

instruction.title: Step 7: Finishing
steps: Serve hot with steamed rice, dosa, or idli.

servingSuggestions.title: Serving Suggestions
items: Pair with dosa, idli, medu vada, or uttapam.
Serve alongside coconut chutney and papad for a complete South Indian meal.

chefTips: Freshly grind the masala – pre-ground powder lacks aroma.
Balance tanginess and heat – add tamarind gradually and adjust chilies for a refined taste.
Ghee over oil – gives richness and a glossy finish.
Cook vegetables separately – keeps textures distinct rather than mushy.
Let it rest for 15 minutes – flavors meld beautifully, just like a slow-cooked restaurant sambar.
